coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence sensing of glutathione based on the in-situ growth of carbon dots-manganese dioxide nanosheets
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Xiaohan
Zhang, Junjie
Zhou, Zitong
Zhou, Zihao
Cheng, Xinyi
Shi, Shaojun
Chen, Jiexia
Ling, Yunyun
coredata.dc:description: Blue fluorescent carbon dots with high purity were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials and then purification by column chromatography. In-situ controllable synthesis was used to prepare carbon dots-manganese dioxide (CDs-MnO2) nanosheets, with carbon dots as the seed, potassium permanganate as the manganese source and sodium citrate as the reducing agent. The as-prepared nanosheets were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, absorption spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. It was found that MnO2 can be degraded by Glutathione (GSH) through a redox reaction, which restores the fluorescence of CDs. A new method for determining GSH was constructed using CDs-MnO2 fluorescence sensing. The method is simple and sensitive, with linear ranges of 0.1–5 μM and 10–60 μM, respectively, and a lowest detection limit of 0.034 μM. The method was successfully used for the determination of GSH in human serum, with satisfactory recoveries in the range of 98.9–100.1%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence sensing
Glutathione
In-situ growth
Carbon dots-manganese dioxide nanosheets

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of synthesized CDs crude solutions at different temperatures. (a) 140 °C; (b) 200 °C; (c) 250 °C.
Morphological characterization of CDs. (a) TEM image; (b) Size distribution (c) HRTEM image.

Optical properties of CDs. (a) Absorption, excitation, and emission spectra (insets are the photographs of the CDs under room light (left) and UV light (365 nm, right)); (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with the excitation of 300–500 nm.

Characterization of CDs-MnO2. (a) Absorption spectra and (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs-MnO2 synthesized by different concentrations of KMnO4; (c) TEM image of CDs-MnO2; (d) Full XPS spectra of the CDs and CDs-MnO2.
Fluorescence spectra of CDs-MnO2 with time in different concentrations of GSH solutions. (a) 2 μM GSH; (b) 20 μM GSH; (c) Reaction kinetic curves of two concentrations of GSH.

Quantitative detection of GSH based on fluorescence intensity changes of CDs-MnO2 probe. (a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs-MnO2 probe in different concentrations of GSH; (b) Linear relationship between the fluorescence enhancement ratio (ΔF/F0) and GSH concentration.
Selectivity of CDs-MnO2 probe for detection of GSH.
Measurement of GSH concentration in 40-folds diluted human serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Fluorescence sensing of glutathione based on the in-situ growth of carbon dots-manganese dioxide nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Wannan Medical College, Wuhu, 241002, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Wannan Medical College, Wuhu 241002, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Synthesis and Application of Medical Materials, Department of Chemistry, Wannan Medical College, Wuhu, 241002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Synthesis and Application of Medical Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Synthesis and Application of Medical Materials, Department of Chemistry, Wannan Medical College, Wuhu, 241002, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Pharmacy, Wannan Medical College, Wuhu, 241002, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blue fluorescent carbon dots with high purity were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials and then purification by column chromatography. In-situ controllable synthesis was used to prepare carbon dots-manganese dioxide (CDs-MnO2) nanosheets, with carbon dots as the seed, potassium permanganate as the manganese source and sodium citrate as the reducing agent. The as-prepared nanosheets were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, absorption spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. It was found that MnO2 can be degraded by Glutathione (GSH) through a redox reaction, which restores the fluorescence of CDs. A new method for determining GSH was constructed using CDs-MnO2 fluorescence sensing. The method is simple and sensitive, with linear ranges of 0.1–5 μM and 10–60 μM, respectively, and a lowest detection limit of 0.034 μM. The method was successfully used for the determination of GSH in human serum, with satisfactory recoveries in the range of 98.9–100.1%.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) is a sulfhydryl molecule that performs important physiological functions. It is a tripeptide formed from glutamic acid, cysteine, and glycine. GSH is widely present in the cells of mammals, plants, and microorganisms (Meister and Anderson, 1983; Sies, 1999; Deneke and Fanburg, 1989; Wu et al., 2004; Sun et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2023). It plays a crucial role in maintaining cellular redox homeostasis, free radical signal transduction, metabolic regulation, gene regulation, and integrative detoxification, among other physiological activities (Smirnova and Oktyabrsky, 2005; Forman et al., 2009). The measurement of GSH content in biological samples is of great interest to chemical researchers. Various methods have been developed for detecting GSH, including high-performance liquid chromatography (Reed et al., 1980; Jia et al., 2014), electrochemical sensing (Harfield et al., 2012; Mahmoud et al., 2023), colorimetry (Duan et al., 2022; Ravikumar et al., 2023), and fluorescence methods (Umezawa et al., 2017; Jiang et al., 2017; Mahmoud et al., 2024). Among these, the fluorescence methods have attracted considerable attention in the field of GSH assay due to its high sensitivity, rapid response, and real-time monitoring capabilities.

Carbon dots (CDs), a novel type of carbon-based nanomaterials, exhibit excellent biocompatibility, low cost, ecofriendliness, tunable surface functions and unique optical properties (Ozyurt et al., 2023; Ahmad et al., 2023). They have great potential for application in fluorescence sensing (Ji et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2022, 2023; Xu et al., 2023; Xie et al., 2024; Li et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2023; Cui et al., 2016). The most commonly used fluorescence sensing mode is the 'turn-on' mode. In this mode, CDs first bind to an energy receptor to quench their fluorescence. Then, the targeted analytes are added to break the combination between CDs and energy receptor, leading to fluorescence recovery (Miao et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2020). One commonly used fluorescence quencher is manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets. MnO2 nanosheets are two-dimensional layered nanomaterials with redox activity, and their basic unit is MnO6 octahedron. The MnO2 nanosheets exhibit a broad absorption spectrum in the range of 200–600 nm, with a strong absorption peak at 380 nm attributed to the d-d transition of Mn ions (Kai et al., 2008; Wang et al., 2003; Gao et al., 2001). This excellent light absorption characteristic makes them suitable for use as a quencher in constructing a fluorescent 'turn-on' sensor.

The preparation of CDs-MnO2 nanoprobe involves several methods, including electrostatic adsorption (Liu et al., 2016a; Lin et al., 2017; Yan et al., 2018; Lu and Xu, 2021), covalent bonding (Yang et al., 2015), conjugation (Liu et al., 2016b), and in situ reaction (Cai et al., 2015; He et al., 2020). Among them, the covalent bonding requires post-modification of the materials separately, which is a cumbersome step and has a long reaction time. However, it should be noted that conjugation is only applicable to CDs with a lamellar structure, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), so there are fewer corresponding literature reports. Electrostatic adsorption is a simple method of combining two materials, but it requires that the materials have opposite charges. For instance, Lin's group (Yan et al., 2018) utilized sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) as the reducing agent and structural modifier, and potassium permanganate (KMnO4) as the manganese source to prepare MnO2 nanosheets with negatively charged surfaces. The positively charged CDs were synthesized directly from m-aminobenzeneboronic acid by a one-step hydrothermal method. Then, the CDs-MnO2 nanoprobe was constructed based on the electrostatic interaction between positively charged CDs and negatively charged MnO2 nanosheets. The in-situ synthesis method avoids modifying nanomaterials. During the preparation process, MnO2 nanosheets are directly connected to CDs, making the nanoprobe more stable and greatly simplifying its preparation process. In this paper, we prepared a high-purity CDs-MnO2 nanoprobe through an in-situ reaction. The nanoprobe achieved highly sensitive detection of the biomolecule GSH through the redox reaction between the MnO2 nanosheets and reducing small molecules. This method has been successfully used to detect GSH in serum.

Ethylenediamine, trisodium citrate and potassium permanganate were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Citric acid anhydrous and glutathione were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). Petroleum ether, ethyl acetate, and methanol anhydrous were bought from Shanghai Titanchem Co., Ltd. (China). All chemicals were used as received without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with double deionized (DI) water (>18 MΩ cm). Serum was collected from the First Affiliated Hospital of Wannan Medical College.

Absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured by a Shimadzu UV-1700 ultraviolet spectrophotometer and a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. Characterizations of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were carried out on a Hitachi HT-7700 electron microscope under the accelerating voltage of 100 kV.

CDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method with slight modifications based on the literature (Song et al., 2015). Anhydrous citric acid (1.1527 g) was weighed and dissolved in 60 ml of water in a 200 ml conical flask. Ethylenediamine (402 μl) was measured and added to the conical flask, followed by thorough shaking. And, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7.0 with NaOH (10 M). Then, the solution was transferred into three Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclaves (25 mL) and heated in a blast style drying oven at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, we collected the brown-black crude products.

The process of purifying CDs involves two steps: roughing filter and column chromatographic separation. In the first step, the crude products were passed through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove large particles. Then, the above solution was purified by column chromatography, employing 300–400 mesh silica gel as the stationary phase and a mixture of methanol and ethyl acetate (3:4) as the mobile phase. The blue-fluorescent solution was obtained by adding the mobile phase. The solvent was then spun dry with a rotary evaporator, and 9 ml of deionized water was added, resulting in an aqueous solution of blue-fluorescence emitting CDs.

2.5 ml of 10 mM sodium citrate solution and 250 μl of 1 mg/ml the aqueous solution of CDs were added into 2.25 ml of DI water under magnetic stirring. Subsequently, 50 μl of 10 mM KMnO4 solution was injected. The mixture was heated to 35 °C and refluxed for 3 h, resulting in CD-MnO2 nanosheets. These processes were repeated several times with concentrations of KMnO4 in the system ranging from 0.1 mM to 0.5 mM.

A set of 5 ml centrifuge tubes were used. DI water (1790 μl), PBS (0.1 M pH = 7.4) (100 μl), CDs-MnO2 (1.25 mg/ml, 100 μl), and varying concentrations of GSH standard solution (10 μl) were added sequentially and mixed thoroughly. At an excitation wavelength of 340 nm, the fluorescence spectra of each solution were measured after 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, separation and characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The experiment investigated the effects of synthesis parameters, including precursor mass, reaction temperature, and reaction time on the resulting products. The results showed that precursor mass and reaction time had a minimal impact on the products, while reaction temperature had a more significant effect (Fig. 1 ). Combined with the literature, a synthesis temperature of 200 °C was chosen to improve the yield of CDs and effectively retain the molecular state luminescence on the surface of CDs.

As shown in Fig. S1, silica gel column chromatography was used for separation and purification crude solution of CDs. Upon addition of the mobile phase, the non-fluorescent and blue fluorescent components were eluted sequentially, resulting in the isolation of blue fluorescent CDs.

The morphological characterization of the blue fluorescent CDs is shown in Fig. 2 . Based on the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 2a), the CDs show monodispersed particles and their sizes in the range of 2.1–3.8 nm with 2.88 nm average size (Fig. 2b), conforming to a normal distribution. Furthermore, the HRTEM image (Fig. 2c) presents a distinct lattice spacing distance of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the d-spacing of the graphene (100) planes.

The optical properties of CDs were investigated through their absorption and fluorescence spectra. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, CDs exhibit good absorption mainly in the UV region (200–400 nm). The absorption peaks at 230–260 nm and 340 nm are assigned to the π-π∗ and n-π∗ transitions of C–C/CC and C–N/C–O, respectively (Jiang et al., 2018). Furthermore, it is obvious that the excitation and emission (excited by 340 nm) spectra of the CDs are almost mirror symmetric each other, and their peaks locate at 336 and 428 nm, respectively. Meanwhile, the aqueous solution of CDs is almost transparent light yellow under room light and emits bright blue fluorescence under irradiation of UV light (365 nm) (inset in Fig. 3a). Fig. 3b displays the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs under various excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence emission peaks of CDs are almost independent of excitation wavelengths, which contradicts many literature reports (Van Dam et al., 2017; Pan et al., 2015; Hu et al., 2015). The CDs in these reports may not have been strictly separated and purified, resulting in products containing multiple components.

It has been reported that the most common method for in-situ synthesis CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites is employing 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES) aqueous buffer (0.1 M, pH 6.0) as reducing agent (Cai et al., 2015; Hiremath et al., 2020, 2021; Hu et al., 2017). However, under this condition the whole reaction system is weakly acidic. Correspondingly, the reaction rate is faster as acidic KMnO4 solution with a strong oxidizing property. Thus, MnO2 nanosheets are easy to self-nucleate and thicker. To achieve high-purity CDs-MnO2 nanosheets, we used CDs as seeds and weakly alkaline sodium citrate as a reducing agent. By adjusting the concentration of KMnO4 in the reaction system, we were able to prepare CDs-MnO2 with varying MnO2 contents in a controlled manner.

Fig. 4 a and b demonstrate that as the concentration of KMnO4 in the reaction system increases, the absorption intensity of CDs-MnO2 in the wavelength range of 250–550 nm increases, meanwhile the fluorescence intensity gradually weakens. Furthermore, the characteristic absorption peak of MnO2 nanosheets was observed at 380 nm (Kai et al., 2008). All above suggest that the MnO2 in CDs-MnO2 increases sequentially, and effectively quenches the fluorescence of CDs. As shown in Fig. 4b, the quenching efficiency of is as high as 90% when the concentration of KMnO4 reaches 0.5 mM. Thus, we chose 0.5 mM KMnO4 for the following preparation of the CDs-MnO2. TEM image (Fig. 4c) confirms the successful preparation of CDs-MnO2, as the CDs are uniformly dispersed on the MnO2 nanosheets. We then employed FTIR and XPS spectra to detect the CDs-MnO2. As shown in Fig. S2, FTIR spectra of the CDs and CDs-MnO2 have the same peaks at 3400, 3250, 1650 and 1556 cm−1, which correspond to the stretching vibrations of O–H, N–H, CO, and the bending vibration of N–H, respectively (Tetsuka et al., 2016; Li et al., 2017). It is important that the CDs-MnO2 displays an obvious Mn–O peak at 526 cm−1 (Lai et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2021). Furthermore, the full XPS spectrum of the CDs-MnO2 indicates the existence of C, O, N, and Mn elements compared with that of CDs (Fig. 4d). As shown in the C 1s spectrum (Fig. S3a), there are three peaks at 284.8, 286.4 and 288.3 eV, which are attributed to C–C/CC, C–N and C–OH/COOH, respectively (Wang et al., 2020). The O 1s spectrum in Fig. S3b shows four peaks at 531.5, 531.7, 533, and 536.1 eV, respectively. The N 1s spectrum of the CDs-MnO2 is similar to that of CDs (Fig. S3c and Fig. S4c), which only has one symmetrical peak at 399.9 eV attributing to amino N as well (Wang et al., 2020). The Mn 2p spectrum exhibits two peaks 642.1 and 653.9 eV, respectively (Fig. S6d). The corresponding spin-energy separation of 11.8 eV indicates the formation of Mn(IV)O2 products (Ling et al., 2019).

In addition, the CDs-MnO2 exhibits very good stability under 25 °C storage condition (Fig. S5). Even under continuous UV light (365 nm) irradiation, the CDs-MnO2 shows excellent photostability (Fig. S6). As shown in Fig. S6b, the fluorescent intensities of the CDs-MnO2 remain almost invariable for 1 h continuous UV light (365 nm) irradiation. The influence of pH of the CDs-MnO2 has been also investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, as medium pH values change from 5.0 to 9.0, both the fluorescent intensities and the emission wavelengths of the CDs-MnO2 remain almost invariable. The fluorescent spectra are almost overlap each other at pH 6.0–7.4 for 15 min incubation.

As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescent intensity of the CDs-MnO2 is recovered significantly with the addition of GSH. However, GSH cannot enhance the fluorescence of CDs. This is because MnO2 can be effectively reduced to Mn2+ through the oxidation of GSH to generate glutathione disulfide (GSSG), as presented in the following equation (Ling et al., 2019).
MnO2 + 2GSH + 2H+ → Mn2+ + GSSG + 2H2O

Thus, we used CDs-MnO2 as a probe and pH 7.4 PBS as a medium (considering the physiological environment of the targeted analyte GSH) to detect GSH. To optimized the time for detecting GSH, we added different concentrations of GSH (2 μM and 20 μM) to the CDs-MnO2 probe solution and monitored the fluorescence spectra in real-time, as shown in Fig. 5 . The reaction occurred rapidly, and the fluorescence enhancement ratio of ΔF/F0 (ΔF=F–F0, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the probe at 428 nm in the absence and presence of GSH, respectively) was immediately enhanced (Fig. 5c). The reaction reached equilibrium at only 11 min, even when the concentration of GSH was as high as 20 μM. To ensure stable signal outputs, the fluorescence spectra were measured after 15 min reaction in the following experiments.

Subsequently, the concentration of GSH was detected using the fluorescence intensity changes of CDs-MnO2 probe at 428 nm under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the probe's fluorescence intensity increased with increasing GSH concentration. Even at a low concentration of 0.1 μM, the fluorescence intensity changes were still observable. Fig. 6b displays the fluorescence enhancement ratio (ΔF/F0) of the probe in the presence of different concentrations of GSH. The relationship between ΔF/F0 and GSH concentration was found to be linear in the ranges of 0.1–5 μM and 10–60 μM, respectively. The corresponding linear equations were ΔF/F0 = 0.1820c- 0.00446 (R2 = 0.9920) and ΔF/F0 = 0.07424c+0.8862 (R2 = 0.9968). The limits of detection (LODs) were 0.034 μM and 3.2 μM, respectively.

To evaluate the selectivity of the CDs-MnO2 probe, a target GSH sample (30 μM) and various co-existing potential interfering substances in serum (ascorbic acid, cysteine, 30 μM; glutamate, histidine, tyrosine, leucine, lysine, glucose, BSA, 300 μM; Na+, Ca2+, Mn2+, 10 mM) were measured separately by this method under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 7 , it is obviously ascorbic acid and cysteine can also cause provide the modest fluorescence enhancement ratio to this probe. The reason is that the sensing detection of GSH by the CDs-MnO2 probe is based on a redox reaction with MnO2 and is not a specific reaction. Fortunately, when GSH coexisted with the aforementioned interferences, there were negligible difference in the ΔF/F0 ratio compared to the control with only GSH added. Furthermore, the concentration of Cys in blood is much lower than that of GSH (with a difference in concentration of about three orders of magnitude) (Cai et al., 2015; Yu et al., 2013; Gao et al., 2016), rendering the interference of Cys negligible.

To further demonstrate the practicability of this sensing platform, the standard addition method was used to determine GSH in human serum. Before analysis, the human serum samples were diluted 40-fold by PBS buffer (5 mM pH = 7.4) to keep GSH concentrations within the linear range of this sensing. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries ranged from 98.9% to 100.1% while RSDs were less than 3.2%. The study demonstrated that the CDs-MnO2 fluorescent probe is suitable for detecting GSH levels in serum.

For this study, we prepared a crude solution of CDs using the hydrothermal method. We obtained high-purity blue CDs through column chromatography separation and purification. Using these CDs as a seed, we in-situ synthesized CDs-MnO2 nanosheets, with control, using KMnO4 as a manganese source and sodium citrate as a reducing agent. CDs-MnO2 was used as a fluorescent probe to construct a highly sensitive GSH fluorescence sensor based on the redox reaction of the active sulfhydryl group of GSH with MnO2. This sensor was successfully used to determine GSH levels in human serum. The nanosheets used in this sensor are simple and convenient to prepare, and the sensor has the advantage of high sensitivity.

Xiaohan Jiang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Junjie Zhang: Investigation, Resources. Zitong Zhou: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zihao Zhou: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xinyi Cheng: Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Shaojun Shi: Investigation, Data curation. Jiexia Chen: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Yunyun Ling: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Higher Education Institutions of China (No. KJ2021A0825), the Excellent Young Talents Fund Program of Higher Education Institutions of Anhui Province (gxyq2022046), National Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training for University of PRC (S202210368124) for financial support of this work.
